audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Numbers / Bemidbar Korah, Dathan, Abiram, censer judgment, and plague-intercession text tradition, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Korah's coalition, holiness challenge, priestly-access test, Dathan and Abiram's counter-accusation, earth-opening and fire judgments, family/household consequences, hammered censers, next-day accusation, plague, and Aaron standing between dead and living. Strong anti-authoritarian, anti-clerical-abuse, anti-collective-punishment, and anti-violence safeguards required.

verses.restored: Korah son of Izhar, son of Kohath, son of Levi, took, with Dathan and Abiram sons of Eliab, and On son of Peleth, sons of Reuben.
They rose up before Moses, with men from children of Israel, two hundred fifty leaders of congregation, summoned ones of assembly, men of name.

They assembled against Moses and Aaron and said to them, Too much for you! For all congregation, all of them, are holy, and YHWH is in their midst. Why then do you lift yourselves above assembly of YHWH?
Moses heard and fell upon his face.
He spoke to Korah and all his congregation, saying, In morning YHWH will make known who is his and who is holy and will bring him near; the one whom he chooses he will bring near to himself.
This do: take for yourselves censers, Korah and all his congregation,
and put fire in them and place incense upon them before YHWH tomorrow. The man whom YHWH chooses, he is holy. Too much for you, sons of Levi!
Moses said to Korah, Hear, please, sons of Levi.

Is it a small thing for you that Divine of Israel separated you from congregation of Israel, to bring you near to himself to serve service of dwelling-place of YHWH and to stand before congregation to minister to them?
He brought you near, and all your brothers sons of Levi with you; and you seek priesthood also?
Therefore you and all your congregation are gathered against YHWH. And Aaron—what is he that you murmur against him?
Moses sent to call Dathan and Abiram sons of Eliab, but they said, We will not come up.
Is it a small thing that you brought us up from land flowing with milk and honey to put us to death in wilderness, that you also make yourself ruler, ruling over us?
Moreover, you have not brought us into land flowing with milk and honey, nor given us inheritance of field and vineyard. Will you gouge out eyes of these men? We will not come up.
Moses became very angry and said to YHWH, Do not turn toward their grain offering. Not one donkey from them have I taken, and I have not harmed one of them.
Moses said to Korah, You and all your congregation, be before YHWH, you and they and Aaron, tomorrow.
Take each man his censer, put incense upon them, and bring near before YHWH each man his censer, two hundred fifty censers; you and Aaron, each man his censer.
They took each man his censer and put fire upon them and placed incense upon them, and stood at entrance of tent of meeting with Moses and Aaron.
Korah assembled against them all congregation at entrance of tent of meeting, and glory of YHWH appeared to all congregation.
YHWH spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying,
Separate yourselves from midst of this congregation, and I will consume them in a moment.
They fell upon their faces and said, El, Divine of spirits of all flesh, shall one man sin/misalign and you be angry against all congregation?
YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,
Speak to congregation, saying, Get yourselves up from around dwelling of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram.
Moses rose and went to Dathan and Abiram, and elders of Israel went after him.
He spoke to congregation, saying, Turn aside, please, from tents of these wicked men, and touch nothing that belongs to them, lest you be swept away in all their sins/misalignments.
They went up from around dwelling of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram. Dathan and Abiram came out standing at entrance of their tents, with their wives, their sons, and their little ones.
Moses said, By this you shall know that YHWH sent me to do all these works, for not from my own heart.
If these die like death of all humanity, and visitation of all humanity is visited upon them, YHWH did not send me.
But if YHWH creates a creation, and ground opens its mouth and swallows them and all that belongs to them, and they go down alive to Sheol, then you shall know that these men spurned YHWH.
As he finished speaking all these words, ground beneath them split apart.
Earth opened its mouth and swallowed them, their households, and every human belonging to Korah, and all possessions.
They and all that belonged to them went down alive to Sheol; earth covered them, and they perished from midst of assembly.
All Israel around them fled at their sound, for they said, Lest earth swallow us.
Fire came out from YHWH and consumed two hundred fifty men bringing near incense.
YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,
Say to Eleazar son of Aaron the priest that he lift up censers from midst of burning, and scatter fire yonder, for they are holy.

Censers of these sinners against their own lives—let them make them into hammered plates as covering for altar, for they brought them near before YHWH and they became holy; they shall be a sign to children of Israel.
Eleazar the priest took bronze censers that those burned had brought near, and they hammered them as covering for altar,

a memorial to children of Israel, so no outsider who is not from seed of Aaron shall draw near to turn incense into smoke before YHWH, and not become like Korah and his congregation, as YHWH spoke by hand of Moses concerning him.
On next day all congregation of children of Israel murmured against Moses and Aaron, saying, You have put to death people of YHWH.
It happened, when congregation assembled against Moses and Aaron, they turned toward tent of meeting, and look, cloud covered it and glory of YHWH appeared.
Moses and Aaron came before tent of meeting.
YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,
Get yourselves up from midst of this congregation, and I will consume them in a moment. And they fell upon their faces.

Moses said to Aaron, Take censer and put fire upon it from altar and place incense, and carry quickly to congregation and make atonement/covering for them, for wrath has gone out from before YHWH; plague has begun.
Aaron took as Moses spoke and ran into midst of assembly, and look, plague had begun among people; he put incense and made atonement/covering for people.
He stood between dead and living, and plague was restrained.
Those dead in plague were fourteen thousand seven hundred, besides those dead concerning matter of Korah.
Aaron returned to Moses at entrance of tent of meeting, and plague was restrained.

translation_notes: Korah's claim that all congregation is holy echoes a genuine Torah principle, but the narrative frames the conflict as a struggle over assigned priestly access and power. This tension must not be flattened.

Do not use the chapter to make leaders immune from criticism. Dathan and Abiram articulate a serious accusation: Moses has not brought the people into promised abundance and appears to rule over them.
Moses and Aaron twice intercede against collective destruction, explicitly asking whether the whole congregation should suffer for one person's sin.
The deaths of households, women, children, and dependents in the earth-opening narrative are severe collective-punishment material requiring explicit ethical critique.
Numbers 26:11 later states that sons of Korah did not die, creating an important textual tension with broad household language here.
The censers become a permanent altar sign, converting instruments of failed rivalry into communal memory.
The next-day accusation—'you have put to death the people of YHWH'—is preserved rather than dismissed, showing continued communal moral protest.
Aaron's final act is not triumph over opponents but intercession: he runs into danger and stands between dead and living.
Plague language is ancient judgment narrative and must not be mapped onto modern illness, epidemics, or moral blame.
safety_note: Do not use Numbers 16 to silence dissent, defend authoritarian or clerical abuse, justify collective punishment, kill religious rivals, blame disease on rebellion, or demand unquestioning obedience. Preserve the narrative's priestly-boundary claim while centering intercession, accountability, the legitimacy of moral protest, and Aaron standing between death and life.
